title: Cost-Share Accountability Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Cost-Share Accountability Act of 2025 makes the Department of Energy report to Congress about cost-sharing in energy projects. Usually, when the government funds energy research and development projects, the people or companies getting the money have to pay for part of the costs themselves. Sometimes the Department of Energy lets them pay less or nothing at all. This law requires the Department of Energy to tell Congress every three months when they reduce or eliminate these cost-sharing requirements and explain why they made those decisions.
last_action_text: Received in the Senate and Read twice and referred to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Cost-Share Accountability Act of two thousand twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2  Reporting Requirements
sections.text: This section changes an existing law called the Energy Policy Act of two thousand five. It adds a new requirement for the Secretary of Energy to create reports. The Secretary must submit the first report no later than one hundred twenty days after this law is passed. After that, the Secretary must submit reports every three months. These reports must go to four committees in Congress. The committees are the Committee on Science, Space, and Technology and the Committee on Appropriations in the House of Representatives. They also go to the Committee on Energy and Natural Resources and the Committee on Appropriations in the Senate. The Secretary must also make these reports available to the public. The reports must explain how the Department of Energy used its power to reduce or eliminate cost-sharing requirements during the time period covered by each report. Cost-sharing requirements mean rules that require people or organizations to pay part of the costs for certain programs or projects.
